posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked:
I find myself looking at CT often to find out when the hell I should be drinking my wines from Burgundy, Bordeaux, and Rhone. Target drinking windows for vintages are vague, and it depends so much on producer and source of fruit (vineyard, etc) as well as how the wine is made, BUT I think the collective here could put together a crowd-think opinion on drinking windows, and perhaps we can create a very useful guide for me and anybody else looking for this info! Categories should be decided by the group as well, but we don’t want them as specific as producer-by-producer, as that will be a long list!

Ideas?

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked: I think if you start one for each category, but break it down like Bordeaux Left Bank, Bordeaux Right Bank, and so on, it’ll be more helpful.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Would be interesting to do. But such a difficult thing to generalize about, as you say. Have been drinking a few 1985 Bordeaux lately, and would consider the 1985 Lafite fully mature and about to decline, the 1985 Haut-Bailly fully mature but holding for a decade, and the 1985 Léoville-Barton mature but with a good twenty-five years ahead of it. Predicting drinking windows might be even harder than scoring wines, if only we would devote as much attention to it.

posts.name: Jay $$ Winton
posts.cooked: and then there’s provenance especially with the older wines.

posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked: Understood it won’t be perfect, so many variables, but as a general guideline, it would be quite helpful! For example, for the last year, most 2008 Burgundies were in a great place for drinking relatively early, but apparently now they have shut down. Another example is that 2005 Bordeaux (other than 1st Growths, mostly) are starting to be good for drinking now, finally.

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked:
I agree that this can be useful. There will always be exceptions, but an overall guide to people’s experiences can be very handy.

I agree with Ian that one thread per region would make it more manageable but that could clutter up the the front page.

posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked:
How about some category suggestions?

Bordeaux Left Bank
Bordeaux Right Bank
Bordeaux First Growth
Burgundy Village
Burgundy Grand Cru
Burgundy Premier Cru
Northern Rhone Hermitage
Northern Rhone Other
Chateauneuf du Pape

Thoughts?

posts.name: Karl K@zaks
posts.cooked:
Suggest also cru Beaujolais and Champagne, then there is also the Loire.

What would be interesting is a bar chart where you could color code the bars and use height of bars two measure two variables: say ultimate potential and current readiness (but could be others).

Then you could scan the chart from left to right and decide on whether to crack the 1955 or 1974.

Also, as William and others mentioned, it really does come down to individual wines and individual bottles.

I myself like to hunt for gems in older vintages thought to be long gone.

posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked:
Suggest also cru Beaujolais and Champagne, then there is also the Loire.

What would be interesting is a bar chart where you could color code the bars and use height of bars two measure two variables: say ultimate potential and current readiness (but could be others).

Then you could scan the chart from left to right and decide on whether to crack the 1955 or 1974.

Also, as William and others mentioned, it really does come down to individual wines and individual bottles.

I myself like to hunt for gems in older vintages thought to be long gone.

This is a broadly general guideline, so no, it can’t be wine or producer specific. Wonder how that bar chart might look…and is there another way to graphically support the data? Something super easy to read on a glance, as we’d have to cover so many vintages as well?

posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked:
Would be interesting to do. But such a difficult thing to generalize about, as you say. Have been drinking a few 1985 Bordeaux lately, and would consider the 1985 Lafite fully mature and about to decline, the 1985 Haut-Bailly fully mature but holding for a decade, and the 1985 Léoville-Barton mature but with a good twenty-five years ahead of it. Predicting drinking windows might be even harder than scoring wines, if only we would devote as much attention to it.

Using your examples wouldn’t 1985 be drink? Aren’t the categories usually:
Hold
Drink/Hold
Drink
Past

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: the drinking windows must be differentiated from maturity windows and, for Burgundy, are both long and variable. I wouldn’t think it helpful. Generalities don’t help and there would need to be too many specifics. But I wish you luck.

posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked:
the drinking windows must be differentiated from maturity windows and, for Burgundy, are both long and variable. I wouldn’t think it helpful. Generalities don’t help and there would need to be too many specifics. But I wish you luck.

How can you say ‘generalities don’t help’? Is not a rough estimate better than none at all, as if someone decides to pull a wine that others have found is shut down hard and either 1) didn’t see a general guideline or 2) did see one and find out that it’s shut down hard, which is more beneficial?

I think crowd sourced data on this is probably a good method, but gathering the information will therefore have to be a bit more complex. We could probably do it with a Google Doc spreadsheet and some fairly simple formulas, and use a vote based system for each region and vintage.

At least using this method there should be some overall commonality, and then generalities will on the whole be roughly correct. It is a guide after all, rather than a gospel truth statement that needs to be adhered to. Individual tastes will always prevail.

It will rely on trust with users not double voting and so on. Maybe we could use our usernames to indicate a vote.

I’ll spend some time thinking about how to put this together. Shouldn’t be too difficult - the issue will be knowing where to curb the scope of the idea!

posts.name: Karl K@zaks
posts.cooked:
This is one of those situations where two contrasting points of views are both not incorrect. So both Alan and Todd are right.

And I would err on the side of doing it rather than not.

But I would like something that would have an “at a glance” aspect to it.

I do like reading detailed threads but I don’t always have the time for it.

Hence the bar chart suggestion. Not saying that is the only option.

For example, I have sporadically bought a variety of cru Beaujolais from 2011 to 2016 and though I am somewhat familiar with vintage assessments I could know more. Are 2011 and 2016 the best for long ageing? Should I be putting them to sleep while opening the others?

I do know how to find more in-depth info but what Todd suggest could help.

Extreme example but cf 94 and 95 Bordeaux - noticeable vintage distinctions.

posts.name: Claus Jeppesen
posts.cooked:
Great to have
But the 25+ years rule is more simple

posts.name: David Glasser
posts.cooked:
the drinking windows must be differentiated from maturity windows and, for Burgundy, are both long and variable. I wouldn’t think it helpful. Generalities don’t help and there would need to be too many specifics. But I wish you luck.

How can you say ‘generalities don’t help’? Is not a rough estimate better than none at all, as if someone decides to pull a wine that others have found is shut down hard and either 1) didn’t see a general guideline or 2) did see one and find out that it’s shut down hard, which is more beneficial?

I agree with Alan and William. Generalizations won’t help because in almost every case you can get more specific recommendations on CellarTracker.

That’s not to say it wouldn’t be fun and a great stimulus for discussion and debate. But I don’t see it as a useful resource unless there are recommendations for specific wines. For that, you need a DB like CT.

posts.name: Brady Daniels
posts.cooked: Will this account for multiple windows per wine? For example, with Burgundy the wines can be great for their first few years, then again after ten or fifteen years. But that window of disappointment in between is a killer.

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked:
Great to have
But the 25+ years rule is more simple

I thought it was the Rule of 15? (for Northern Rhone)

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked:
Will this account for multiple windows per wine? For example, with Burgundy the wines can be great for their first few years, then again after ten or fifteen years. But that window of disappointment in between is a killer.

I would be cool if we could graph these hiccups out, something similar to what Jancis Robinson did in her Vintage Timelines book, because wine doesn’t always age in a linear fashion.
